[271:33]As founding members of the United Nations itself, France and the United Kingdom are

[271:39]permanent members on the Security Council.

[271:42]Why is this important?

[271:51]The United Nations has divided up into many different bodies.

[271:54]The biggest of which is the General Assembly, where all the countries, all 193 sovereign

[272:00]countries come together and they vote on resolutions.

[272:04]But everything that the General Assembly passes is non-binding.

[272:08]The Security Council on the other hand, their directives which they pass are binding.

[272:17]Its members have a responsibility to carry them out.

[272:22]Unlike the General Assembly, their resolutions are not binding.

[272:27]Now, because the Security Council is such an important institution, it has five members

[272:33]that sit on the Security Council permanently.

[272:37]The rest of the seats are on a rotating basis.

[272:40]The five permanent members, which are all nuclear powers, the United States, France,

[272:44]the United Kingdom, China, and Russia, they all have veto power over everything that the

[272:50]Security Council considers.

[272:56]They are nuclear powers, and they have veto power over everything the Security Council

[273:01]passes.

[273:03]And this is to achieve balance.

[273:06]So in the days of the Cold War, of course, the United States would want to pass something

[273:11]in the Security Council going to war with Vietnam, let's say, or Korea would be a good

[273:17]example.

[273:18]But that, of course, would have been vetoed by the Soviet Union or by China on the Eastern

[273:24]In modern times, it's the same way.

[273:31]Russia and China can veto it and balance.

[273:42]and balance it out in the other direction.
